title: Learning to do nothing after a lifetime of hypervigilance
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
The unbearable stillness of being 

 I sat on my couch for forty-seven minutes yesterday. Not reading. Not scrolling. Not thinking about anything in particular...
metaDescription:
The unbearable stillness of being 

 I sat on my couch for forty-seven minutes yesterday. Not reading. Not scrolling. Not thinking about anything in parti...
htmlContent:
The unbearable stillness of being

I sat on my couch for forty-seven minutes yesterday. Not reading. Not scrolling. Not thinking about anything in particular. Just sitting. And I thought I was going to crawl out of my skin.

Here's the thing. My body doesn't know how to do nothing. It never learned. From the time I was small, stillness meant danger. Quiet meant someone was about to explode. Relaxation was a trap you fell into right before the floor dropped out from under you.

I'm guessing you know what I'm talking about.

Hypervigilance isn't just being alert. It's a full-body state where your nervous system is scanning for threats at all times, even when you're supposedly resting. Your eyes are tracking movement. Your ears are picking up every creak. Your muscles are holding tension you don't even feel anymore. And your mind? It's running scenarios, calculating exits, preparing for the worst.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, would say this is your sympathetic nervous system doing its job. It's keeping you alive. But here's the problem: your system doesn't know the threat is over. It doesn't know you're safe now. It's still running the old program because nobody ever taught it a new one.

Sit with that for a second.

Your nervous system is doing exactly what it was designed to do. It's just that the design was made for a war that ended years ago. Maybe decades ago. Maybe you were a child then. Maybe the war was your home, your family, your own bedroom.

And now someone tells you to just relax. Just breathe. Just be still.

And your body screams: Are you insane? That's how people die.

Something I think about often is the concept of "good enough." Not perfect healing. Not complete resolution. Just good enough. Winnicott used this phrase for parenting - the "good enough mother." I think it applies to recovery too. Good enough recovery. Where you're not cured but you're not drowning. Where you still get triggered but you know what to do. Where you still have hard days but they don't define you.

Deb Dana calls this "glimmers" - those small moments of ventral vagal activation. A warm cup of tea. A genuine laugh. A moment of feeling connected. They're not dramatic. They're not Instagram-worthy. But they're real. And they add up. Every glimmer is a deposit in your nervous system's safety account. Every one matters.

Right now, can you think of one glimmer from today? One moment - even half a second - where your body felt okay? Where you weren't bracing or scanning or performing? Hold that. Let it land. Let your body register that it happened. Because your nervous system has a negativity bias - it remembers threats much more easily than safety. So you have to actively help it notice the good. Not to bypass the hard stuff. But to balance it. To remind your body that safety exists too. Even if it's just for a moment.

What doing nothing actually asks of you

I used to think doing nothing meant lying on the couch watching Netflix. But that's not nothing. That's distraction. That's numbing. That's keeping your brain occupied so you don't have to feel what's actually there.

Real doing nothing is something else entirely. It's sitting with your eyes open and your hands still and your attention turned inward. It's letting yourself feel the weight of your body against the chair. It's noticing the tightness in your jaw, the shallow breath in your chest, the faint tremor in your hands.

And this is where it gets hard. Because when you stop running, you start feeling.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. It's not just a memory in your head. It's a physical imprint. It's held in your fascia, your muscles, your nervous system. And when you finally stop moving, stop doing, stop achieving, stop surviving - that trapped energy starts to stir.

I remember the first time I tried to just sit. I lasted maybe three minutes before I jumped up and started cleaning the kitchen. My body was screaming at me. My mind was generating urgent tasks. There was a voice in my head saying you're wasting time, you're being lazy, something bad will happen if you stop.

That voice isn't you. It's the hypervigilance talking. It's the part of you that learned, through real and painful experience, that stillness equals vulnerability. That part kept you alive. It deserves gratitude, not shame.

But it also needs to learn something new.

The resistance is the point

I want to be really honest with you about something. When I started trying to do nothing, I failed. A lot. I'd set a timer for five minutes and by minute two I'd be reaching for my phone. I'd try to meditate and end up planning my entire week. I'd lie down to rest and my brain would start replaying every embarrassing thing I'd said since 2003.

I thought I was bad at resting. Turns out I was just bad at sitting with my own discomfort.

And that's exactly the point. The discomfort is the work.

Look, I know this is heavy material. And I know there's a part of you that might be thinking "this is too much" or "I can't handle this" or "maybe I should just close this tab." That part is trying to protect you. And that's okay. But I want you to know that you can handle this. You've already handled worse. The fact that you're here, reading this, means your survival system did its job. You made it.

What we're doing here isn't about reliving the past. It's about understanding it well enough to stop being controlled by it. There's a difference between being trapped in a memory and looking at it from a safe distance. You get to choose how close you get. You get to choose when to step back. That choice - that agency - is itself a form of healing.

When you've lived in hypervigilance for years, your nervous system has a very narrow window of tolerance. That's Dan Siegel's term for it - the zone where you can be present without getting flooded or shutting down. For people like us, that window is tiny. Anything outside of it feels like an emergency.

Doing nothing stretches that window. Not by forcing it open, but by letting you sit at the edges. You feel the urge to run. You notice the panic rising. And you stay. Not through gritted teeth, but with curiosity. Oh, there it is. That's the part that thinks we're in danger. Hi, friend.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker gets into the specifics of what this looks like - the freeze response, the inner critic, the ways we keep ourselves trapped in survival mode. He doesn't tell you to just relax. He tells you why you can't, and then walks you through what to do about it.

I read that book with a highlighter in one hand and a box of tissues in the other. It helped me understand that my inability to rest wasn't a personal failing. It was a symptom. And symptoms can be healed.

What your body actually needs

Here's something nobody told me: doing nothing doesn't mean forcing yourself to be calm. It means giving your body permission to be exactly as it is, without trying to change it.

If you're shaking, you shake. If you're crying, you cry. If you're so tense you feel like a coiled spring, you just notice that tension without trying to release it. The release comes on its own, in its own time, when your system finally understands that it's safe enough to let go.

Think about it. You've spent years training your body to stay on alert. You can't undo that in a week. You can't undo it by forcing yourself to breathe slowly while your whole system is screaming danger. That's just another form of control. Another way of saying your body is wrong for feeling what it feels.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). Tara Brach talks about this exact thing - the way we fight our own experience, the way we judge ourselves for not being further along, the way we make healing into another performance. Her work helped me stop trying to fix my nervous system and start just being with it.

I'm not saying it's easy. I'm saying it's real.

Start small. Like, embarrassingly small. Two minutes of sitting. One minute of lying on the floor with your eyes closed. Just noticing what your body does when you stop. Not judging it. Not fixing it. Just witnessing.

I want to talk about something that doesn't get said enough in recovery spaces. Healing can feel like loss. When you start to let go of your survival strategies - the hypervigilance, the people-pleasing, the emotional walls - it can feel like you're losing yourself. Because those strategies were you, for a very long time. They were how you knew yourself.

Gabor Mate writes about this beautifully. The true self that got buried under survival adaptations - it's still there. But meeting it can be disorienting. Who am I without the anxiety? Who am I without the need to control everything? Who am I when I'm not performing for someone else's approval? These are terrifying questions. And they don't have quick answers.

But here's what I've found. On the other side of that disorientation is something real. Something that doesn't need to perform or protect or prove. Something that can just be. It's quiet. It's simple. And it's been waiting for you this whole time. You don't have to find it all at once. You just have to be willing to keep looking. Even when it's scary. Especially when it's scary.

Your body might jerk. You might feel a wave of heat. You might get nauseous. You might fall asleep. All of that is okay. All of that is information.

Your body has been carrying this for a long time. It deserves to be heard.

The return to yourself

I've been practicing doing nothing for about two years now. Some days I can sit for twenty minutes. Some days I can't do two. The progress isn't linear. It's not supposed to be.

But something has shifted. I notice it in small moments. Like when I'm waiting for my coffee to brew and I don't reach for my phone. Or when I'm driving and I realize I've been relaxed for a whole minute without scanning for danger. Or when I lie down at night and my jaw isn't clenched. Just a little. Just enough to notice.

My nervous system is learning. Slowly. Painfully. Beautifully.

It's learning that stillness isn't death. That quiet isn't a precursor to violence. That I can sit in my own body and not be annihilated.

That's the return. Not to some idealized version of myself who meditates for an hour every morning and never feels anxious. But to the self who can feel her own breath. Who can sit with her own pain. Who can be present for her own life instead of just surviving it.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz's Internal Family Systems model helped me understand that the part of me that can't stop scanning for threats isn't broken. It's just doing a job it learned a long time ago. And it can learn a new job. It can learn to rest.

I don't know where you are in this process. Maybe you're reading this while scrolling on your phone, avoiding the feeling in your chest. Maybe you're crying. Maybe you're numb. Maybe you're angry that someone is even suggesting you do nothing when you have so much to do.

I see you. I've been there. I'm still there, some days.

But I want you to know that the restlessness, the boredom, the panic, the rage - none of it means you're doing it wrong. It means you're doing it. It means you're finally letting your system know that you're ready to listen.

And that's the beginning of everything.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link)
